fuzzy_prd:
We need a small library that takes a partially-filled cluster deployment specification and turns it into a complete, validated one, so operators can declare only the fields they actually care about and trust the rest to be filled in consistently. The cluster spec describes two cooperating groups — a primary data store and a monitoring/sentinel component — each with a container image and a number of replicas. The primary group also has a metrics-exporter image, and the sentinel group can carry a list of free-form configuration directives. When any of these fields are missing or have non-positive values, the library should substitute sensible built-in defaults. We also need the library to derive the deterministic names of the infrastructure resources the cluster creates, so that separate tools always agree on what each resource is called. Resource names are built from a fixed product prefix plus per-component tokens. The primary component's shutdown configuration object has an additional override mechanism: a caller can pin an explicit name, otherwise the library falls back to the auto-derived name — handle this the same way the existing operator naming conventions work. We also need a label-map merger: given an ordered list of label maps, merge them left to right where later values override earlier ones, skip any absent entries, and return the combined labels sorted alphabetically. Finally the library must validate the cluster name before doing any defaulting: if the name is too long to be used as a resource identifier, reject it immediately with a clear error.

One extra pass of detail from the team’s questions: the built-in image defaults should be exactly redis image = 'redis:5.0-alpine', redis exporter image = 'oliver006/redis_exporter:v0.33.0', and sentinel image = 'redis:5.0-alpine'. The default replica count is 3 for both redis and sentinel, and that same fallback applies when the field is missing or the provided value is non-positive, just like the original ticket says.

Also, for sentinel config, the default custom_config list is exactly ['down-after-milliseconds 5000', 'failover-timeout 10000']. When this is emitted in the completed spec/output, those entries are joined with '|' as the separator, so the resulting value is exactly 'down-after-milliseconds 5000|failover-timeout 10000'.

And on name validation, the cutoff is 48 characters. If the cluster name is longer than 48 characters, reject it right away with 'error=name_too_long\nmax_length=48\n' and do not perform any defaulting first.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: func GenerateRedisName(name string) string; func GenerateSentinelName(name string) string; func GenerateRedisShutdownName(name string) string; func GetRedisShutdownConfigMapName(rf *RedisFailover) string

trigger_keywords: what prefix and tokens are used for resource names
how are the resource names constructed
what is the exact naming formula for each component
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Resource names use prefix 'rf': redis = 'rfr-<name>' (token 'r'), sentinel = 'rfs-<name>' (token 's'), redis_shutdown = 'rfr-s-<name>' (token 'r-s').

trigger_keywords: how are merged labels ordered in the output
in what order are label keys emitted
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The merged label map is emitted with keys sorted lexicographically (alphabetical order), one 'key=value' line per entry.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: handle this the same way the existing operator naming conventions work
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The GetRedisShutdownConfigMapName function in the golden source returns the explicit shutdown_configmap value if set and non-empty, otherwise falls back to GenerateRedisShutdownName(rf.Name) which produces 'rfr-s-<name>'

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that detail. Please refer to the start.md specification and the public test cases in rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ for authoritative requirements.

feature1_spec_defaulting.json.description: Apply defaulting and validation to a deployment specification. Missing or non-positive values are filled with built-in defaults. A name longer than max length is rejected.

feature2_1_resource_names.json.description: Derive the deterministic resource names for a cluster from its base name.

feature2_2_shutdown_configmap_name.json.description: Resolve the shutdown configmap name: explicit override if provided, else auto-derived.

feature3_merge_labels.json.description: Merge an ordered list of label maps left-to-right. Later values override earlier ones. Null entries are skipped. Output is sorted by key.
